sentences.text: Yeah.
OK?
Yeah.
Well, that, that was a death march.
Many, many, many people died on the way.
And people that, that were weak and could not walk fast enough were just shot and left on the roadside.
We finally arrived in the town of Breslau in Germany.
And there, they put us on trains, on open, open coal cars.
We were-- they put about 100 people in, in one car like that.
And that, that train arrived in-- we, we were, we stayed-- incidentally, the train ride that, that we were on there took about four days or five days and four nights.
It went-- the train went back and forth, back and forth.

They needed the, when they needed that locomotive to, to pull another train, a troop train or a transport train, they would just disconnect the locomotive and leave our train standing on the, on the siding.
And we had no, no food and no water.
It was, it was winter.
It was a terrible thing.
Many people died on that train.
But finally, it arrived in, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in, in Breslau.
They brought us to a camp, Gross-Rosen, called Gross-Rosen.
And the condition in that camp were very, very bad.
It was overcrowded.
The facilities were not there.
There were, there were just none.
There was a-- they put us up in barracks that were already overcrowded.
We could not even lay down and sleep.
We had to sit in there, sit up.
We could not even stretch out to lay.
If somebody had to go out to relieve himself, he had to step over, step over the bodies of the others.
It was terrible condition.
Well, after a day or two, they called out that they needed mechanics.
So we, of course, came from this factory.
We all volunteered.
We, we figured that the faster we get out of this camp, it would be better for us.
If we could get back to work in a factory, we would get at least some rations.
And we would be protected.
So we volunteered.
They needed 200.
So we got, we got 200 together.
And we marched about 20 kilometers to a town called Reichenau.
No, the town was called Bolkenhain, Bolkenhain That was the place where they took us.
And when we got there, there was a small camp, a very small camp.
It had about 200, 300 people in there.
But those people looked like they were walking skeletons.
Even we that came from Auschwitz, we had never seen anything like that.
It was just unbelievable.
So we asked these people, what happened?
Where's the factory?
Where do you work?
So they told us, oh, they haven't worked in weeks.
There was a factory.
It's nearby.
But it hasn't been functional because it has been bombed.
And they, they don't give us any food.
They just keep us here.
And they were actually walking skeletons, starving.
And soon, we found out what they actually wanted us to do.
They took us-- the next morning, they took us out into a clearing, into the woods and then into a clearing, clear area.
And they had us dig ditches.
And they told us that those ditches were like foxholes for anti-aircraft protection.
In case there is an air raid, we could jump in those foxholes and be protected.
So we were digging those ditches all day long.
And in the evening, they took us back to this camp in Bolkenhain.
And when we got back, all those people that were there that we saw, those skeletons, they were all dead.
They had injected them with, with air in the veins and killed them all.
And our job was to bury them in those ditches that we dug.
That's why they took us over there.
So they wrapped each body up in a blanket.
And four prisoners had to carry one, one body out into the woods there in the clearing and bury them.
And then by the time we got back to the camp, it was morning already.
That was all done during the night.
And then in the morning when we got back, it was dawn already.
The camp was empty.
There was no prisoners there.
But the guards had loaded all their belongings on wagons.
See, they were going to evacuate this camp but they had no trucks and no cars, because all the trucks and cars were all confiscated for the army.
So they had wagons.
And they wanted us to push those wagons with their belongings further west.
So that's what we had to do.
They evacuated us.
And we had to go and push those wagons all day long to further west, constantly going west.
Because they did not want to fall into Russian hands.
See, they, if they were going to be captured, they'd rather be captured by the Americans or the English, not by the Russians.
They didn't like the Russians.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So finally, we ended up in a town of Hirschberg, which is also in Germany, near the Czechoslovakian border.
And there was also another camp.
From that camp, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we went to a town called Reichenau.
And in Reichenau, they put us again on trains, open cattle cars, open cattle cars.
Again, they put about 100 to 120 into one car.
They put a bucket in the middle for sanitary, to relieve yourself, no food.
This was in February.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, this train moved also back and forth for seven days and six nights.
It finally arrived in Buchenwald, in the concentration camp of Buchenwald.
But when it arrived there, from those 100, 120 people that were on that train, only seven of us were alive.
They actually all starved or frozen to death.
What we had done, is somebody died, we moved the bodies away to one side.
And we took off their clothing and put the clothing around us to keep warm.
And when it arrived in Buchenwald, they opened up the gates, the, the, the doors of the cars, there were prisoners there that opened up the, the doors.
And like I said, only seven of us were alive.
And we could hardly walk.
We were all frozen.
Feet were frozen, everything.
The toes just fell off from the frostbites.
So the, the, the kapos and, and the administration of the camp from Buchenwald was again run by prisoners, but not like in Auschwitz by, by, by criminal prisoners, but by political prisoners.
These were German political prisoners, like intellectuals, democrats, socialists, communists.
And these people, of course, were very nice people.
And they were trying to help us as much as they could.
So they unloaded this train.
And they took us right away to the [GERMAN SPEECH], which was the sick bay.
I was on block 20-- block 61, which was the sick bay there.
The, the beds, again, in there were like planks, four one on top of each other and four levels.
Again, four or five people were laying on one, on one bunk with no mattresses or anything, just some straw they had in there.
And we were, of course, very sick.
They did not give us much food there, because the commandant of the camp there in Buchenwald said that the sick people don't need the full ration.
They only need the soup.
They didn't give us any bread, because we are not productive.
We cannot work, so we don't need the full ration.
So you can visualize our condition.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In, In Buchenwald, there was a, there was a doctor, so-called doctor.
We found out that he was really a butcher in private life.
He was a butcher.
And he was the surgeon.
He did the operations in Buchenwald, in the, in that block 61.
His name was Herman, incidentally.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was a political prisoner.
And he did the best he could.
He amputated all the toes that were frozen.
And some had to be legs amputated and whatever had to be done.
They had no bandages.
They had only paper.
They bandage it up with paper-- no medicine, nothing.
Well, I was only a few weeks in Buchenwald.
When I arrived in Buchenwald in, in March, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on March 7th, I arrived in Buchenwald.
And in April 11, the American Army advanced and liberated Buchenwald.
If it would have lasted a few day longer, few days more, I don't think I would have survived.
I was so bad that when the American soldiers came in, I was asking my friends, who are these guards that are going to guard us now?
We didn't realize that these were our liberators.
Now, the American army, of course, took the sick people out of the camp.
They took the sick ones out first from the camp.
And they took us Weimar.
Weimar is the town next to Buchenwald, the closest town next to Buchenwald.
And they put this up in hospitals where the-- German hospitals-- where the doctors-- they had real doctors and nurses.
And they put us up in clean beds.
And they took care of us in there.

We were very fortunate that we were taken out and put into these hospitals, because the other people that were, that were a little better off that could walk, many of those people-- thousands of them, I would say-- died after the liberation.
Because the soldiers that came in, they wanted to help us.
And they didn't know, they didn't realize, that we could not eat regular food.
So there were giving us all their food, their rations-- sausages, butter, and all the rations that they had.
But the prisoners that were so many years in that camp could not digest that kind of food because all their intestines were dried up.
So we that were in the hospital, the doctors, they realized this.
So they did not give us food right away.
They gave us very little food, special food that they made for us, like cereal cooked with skim milk and water.
And slowly, every day, they gave us a little better food till finally, we were able to eat normal food.
Now, while we were in this hospital, the different countries-- Holland, Belgium, and France-- they had sent delegations into Buchenwald to look for their citizens.
Because many Belgian citizens and Dutch citizens and French citizens, they were in the camps.
So the Belgian delegation wanted to know which Belgian citizens were there, were alive, so they could repatriate them.
So I said a little lie.
I told him that I was a Belgian citizen because I came originally-- I came from Belgium.
So I gave them my last address where I stayed.
And I spoke a little French.
And I spoke Flemish.
And they accepted it.
So they registered my name as a Belgian citizen.
And by May 5th, they took us in army transport planes and flew us back to Brussels.
At the airport, before we boarded the planes, they put us on stretchers.
And they had the stretchers stacked up inside the plane for us to, to lay there.
But before we boarded the train, the plane, they took all our clothes off.
And they disinfected us with a disinfectant solution.
And they gave us clean uniforms.
They gave us German uniforms, German army uniforms-- new, brand new ones.
That's the only thing they had.
Because they wanted us to be clean.
See, they, they were afraid that we were bringing back lice, you know, even in the hospital there.
Because all the thing-- the sanitary condition in the camp was so bad that the-- they were afraid that we would bring the lice back to, to Belgium and to civilian life.
Well, anyway, they brought us back to Belgium.
We came into Brussels.
And at the airport, they waited for us with rows of ambulances.
And they took us into hospitals in, in Brussels.
I myself was brought into a hospital in Brussels.
And the Belgian doctors, of course, took very good care of us.
And the Belgian population was very good to us.
They came and visited us and brought us flowers and brought us candy.
And, you know, they wanted to be nice to us.
And I had a friend that lived in Belgium, Johnny Heller.
He had joined the Resistance in Belgium, the resistance, the Belgian resistance.
And when the war was over, he volunteered to work in the committee, in that committee that registered all the people that came back from the camps.
So when he found my name there, he came right away to the hospital to visit me.

So-- and through him then and through the Belgian Jewish committee that was formed then, the Joint Distribution Committee sent a committee from America, sent it over there to help the people that were liberated, to help them to get settled again.
So through them, we found my brother.
My brother was, was able to hide in Belgium in the Ardennes, in the Belgium mountains.
He managed to stay in hiding all throughout the war.
We also was able to get in touch with my uncle that was in New York, in the United States.
He-- as a matter of fact, he himself had made inquiries through the communities in, in New York to look for us.
And we in turn did it from Belgium.
So we found each other.
And we also found my mother in England.
But I was still very weak in the hospital.
So when the time came that they said that I don't need to be in the hospital anymore, they took me out from the hospital.
And they brought us to a convalescent home in nearby Brussels.
That was a home that was donated by the queen of Belgium.
The queen mother, Elizabeth, donated that home for people like us to recuperate in.
And I was in that home for almost a year to-- because I had to learn how to walk.
And I had to, you know, to get my, my weight back and my strength.
And in that home, of course, we were, we were totally on our own.
We were free to go in and out like, like anybody else.
You know, we were not imprisoned in that home.
The home was very good to us.
And during all that time, we-- of course, I corresponded with my family.
And my brother had decided-- yeah, my uncle that was in America here, he also had another brother that lived here in America.
He lived in New Jersey.
And he had a farm.
And he sent us papers to come here to the United States.
And my brother did not want to come to the United States.
He, he became-- while he was in hiding in, in the Ardennes there, he became a Zionist.
And he wanted to go to Palestine.
And of course, then in those days, there was no state.
So the only way you could go to then Palestine was illegally.
So he managed to go to Marseilles, to France.
And they went in one of those illegal boats that went to Palestine.
That particular boat that he was on got caught by the British, British patrol outside of Haifa.
They, they took the-- all the people that were on that boat.
They were interned in Cyprus.
And-- but my brother was able to get out from Cyprus, because my father was in Israel.
He lived in Haifa at that time.
He got in touch with him.
And because he was a resident, an official resident by that time-- he had lived there since 1936 or 1937.
And now it was 1946.
So he lived there already for 10 years.
So he was a regular legal resident.
He was able to get him out of Cyprus.
And he went to, to live there near my father.
I myself came-- went to-- came to America.
And we-- I, I settled in New Jersey, in New Brunswick.
And I stayed with my uncle that had this farm.
We stayed there.
I stayed there.
But the farm life did not agree with me.
I wanted to live in a big city, because I came from a big city.
So I came to New York.
And the uncle that I had here in New York, he had no children.
But he had an apartment.
And he had a small store in Queens here.
And he told me that look, you can stay in my apartment as long as you want.
You look around, find a job.
And when you get settled, you go on your own.
In the meantime, I had a place to stay, which was, which was true.
And I, I went around every day.
I met other friends that were together with me in Auschwitz.
And we went together.
We went downtown to the HIAS to-- we told them that look, we, we just want a job.
My friend that went with me, he was in a similar position as I was.
He also got-- had an uncle in the United States that got him to come here.
See, we did not come on a, on a refugee visa from the HIAS.
We came as individual immigrants.
And so we told the HIAS that we don't need any help, financial help.
All we need is a job, that they should give us a job.
Well, they said, OK, come back in a week or so.
And we will see what we can do.
So when we came back, he said-- they told us, look, we have a job for you.
But the job is in, in Providence, Rhode Island.
And we will help you to go there.
We will give you money, car fare to go there.
We will give you money to rent a room in a rooming house and buy food.
And then once you, once you start to work and you earn money, you pay back the money.
And that's how we will help you.
But there's one catch to it.
What's the catch?

They said, since we did not bring you here, you did not come here on this, on this, on our visa, on our refugee visa, see, a displaced person visa-- yeah, this was-- President Truman at the time had passed legislation that he would take 100,000 displaced person to the United States, see?
And they were here, were many of them here in New York.
And the HIAS vouched for them that they would not be a burden to the state, see?
But myself and my friend, we did not come under that displaced person act.
We didn't fall under that.
So therefore, they wanted us to bring them a paper from my uncle to state that he can no longer take care of us.
And that then, then, therefore, they will vouch for us.
So I, of course, could not ask my uncle to do this.
Because after all, he did take care of us.

He sent me the money, the car fare, which was $125 at the time, to come here with the ship, which was the Marine Falcon, which was one of those liberty ships that they built at the time to bring troops, to transport troops, and to transport goods, you know, to bring back and forth to Europe and to bring to Russia to help the war.
So-- but I could not ask him for such a paper.
You know, that would be not right, you know?
So I told him, no, I cannot give him that paper.
So therefore, they would not give me the job.
And we went out on our own.
And we found-- finally found jobs, odd jobs, any kind of a job.
I worked in a cleaning store.
I was running an elevator.
I worked in a factory.
Any kind of a job we did, just to get us some money and, and help us out.
And then, of course, we were young.
You know, I was, at that time I was 20-- 24, 23, 24 years old.
And later on, of course, we found better jobs.
We earned more money.
And my uncle that, that I was staying with, he had found a, a bigger apartment near his business.
So he moved out of his apartment.
And he left me his apartment where he was staying in Washington Heights.
And by that time, I had met my wife.
We were going out.
We were going to get married.
So right away, I had an apartment to stay.
And that's how I, I met my wife then, as I said.
And we got married.
And we lived in that apartment.
And we got married in 1949.
And then after about approximately two years, we bought a house here in Flushing, this same house here where we are now.
And we moved here to Flushing.
And then after another couple-- no, when we moved here, my wife was pregnant already.
My, my daughter was born here in, in this house-- I mean, in a hospital.
But we stayed here in this house, my older daughter.
And then about two years later, my other daughter was born, also here.
And so I said-- like I said, I have two children.
And--
The older one name is Heidi.
And the younger one is Elizabeth.
My wife's name is Laurie.
And I have four grandchildren.
My older daughter has one child.
Her name is Amanda.
She's three years old.
My younger daughter has three children.
Their names are Jonathan, who is 11 years old, and his sister, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Allison, who is 10 years old.
And then the younger one is Shawn, who is six years old.
And my daughters, both my daughters went to college.
My older daughter is-- has an MBA, a master's degree in business administration.
She works in Washington for the Postmaster General's office.
My younger daughter also went to college.
And she majored in, also in business.
But she majored in accounting.
And later, she had a job in-- with an accounting firm.
But then when she had the children, of course, she's not working.
Because she has a full time job, take care of three children.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I go into the--
Well, I tell you, in the first early years, I could not talk about what had had happened in the-- during the Holocaust.
It was many years.
As a matter of fact, it was actually only till 1981.
It was almost 36 years till we actually opened up and could really talk freely about it.
At that time, there was a gathering of Holocaust survivors in Jerusalem.
And there was about 10,000 survivors that went to Jerusalem, including myself and my wife.
We went to Jerusalem.
And we participated in this gathering.
And this was such a powerful gathering.
There was people from all over the world.
There were survivors that came from South Africa, from Brazil, from everywhere, all around the world-- from European countries, across the United States, from Canada, Argentina, Brazil.
And we met many people that we knew in the camps.
And we recognized each other.
And we really-- it was a, a gathering that gave us a relief.
